This is specifically for the case when a subsegment is added to an unsampled subsegment. We have to ensure that once an unsampled subsegment is created, all of its child subsegments are also unsampled. 

So for example, it is possible to do this:
```
    segment = Segment('parent_segment', 'id', 'id', True)
    subsegment = Subsegment('subsegment1', 'local', segment)
    subsegment.sampled = False
    segment.add_subsegment(subsegment)
    subsegment2 = Subsegment('subsegment2', 'local', segment)
    subsegment.add_subsegment(subsegment2)
```
In this case, it would be illegal for `subsegment2` to be sampled, so it has to take the decision of it's direct parent subsegment - `subsegment1`- which is unsampled.

```py
    ...: from pandas.core.construction import extract_array
    ...: from pandas.core.dtypes.common import is_extension_array_dtype

    ...: 
    ...: res = operator.le(s1, 2)
    ...: display(res)  # array([ True,  True, False, False])

Out[20]: True
```

Basically here in the code we are iterating over all Server sub-TLVs:
- We first check if the enclosing Service TLV does contain IPv6 address, if so, we count it as one entry (each server TLV represents one RLOC (one BR) supporting this address). 
- Then we also check if the "server sub-TLV" has an address embedded in its data and if it does, we count it as an entry as well.

On `master`, here's how `_EarlyStoppingCallback` currently populates `best_score_list`.

The first time the callback is called, run this `_EarlyStoppingCallback._init()` method.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/2fe2bf0675ef127a29f9bc49511fc0099cf3e140/python-package/lightgbm/callback.py#L348-L350

In that `_init()` method, ensure that `self.best_score_list` is the same length as `CallbackEnv.evaluation_result_list` by padding it with `None`s.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/2fe2bf0675ef127a29f9bc49511fc0099cf3e140/python-package/lightgbm/callback.py#L328-L330

And then whenever the callback is called, iterate over `CallbackEnv.evaluation_result_list` and put the corresponding item from it into `self.best_score_list` if:

* it's the very first time the callback was called (`self.best_score_list[i] is None`)
* the new evaluation result is better than the previous one by at least some margin `delta`, which defaults to 0.0 (`self.cmp_op[i](score, self.best_score[i])`

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/2fe2bf0675ef127a29f9bc49511fc0099cf3e140/python-package/lightgbm/callback.py#L355-LL358

So those `None` entries in `self.best_score_list` aren't expected to be there when the first call of `_EarlyStoppingCallback` completes. They're not visible to user code, and nothing else in `lightgbm` relies on them.

HOWEVER... their brief presence in that list tells `mypy` "well sometimes there can be `None` in this list". And honestly, it confused me too.

This PR proposes making `self.best_score_list` easier for both `mypy` and humans to understand by removing that `None`-padding, and instead changing the update logic to *"if it's the first time this list is being updated, build it up by `.append()`-ing every element of `env.evaluation_result_list` to it"*.

Indeed, the `sums`, `means`, `std` matrices are not symmetric, however, the `sum_of_pairwise_mul` and `count` matrices are always symmetric. From these facts, we can infer that the resulted correlation matrix is also always symmetrical:

Consider this formula for the correlation coefficient:
```
top[a, b] = mul[a, b] - mean[b, a] * sum[a, b] - mean[a, b] * sum[b, a] + count[a, b] * mean[a, b] * mean[b, a]
down[a, b] = std[a, b] * std[b, a]
corr[a, b] = top[a, b] / down[a, b]
```
As it's seen, the `down[a, b] == down[b, a]`. As we know that `mul` and `count` are symmetric matrices, let's replace their components with simply `M` and `C`, then we will have the following expression for the `top[a, b]`:
```
top[a, b] = M - mean[b, a] * sum[a, b] - mean[a, b] * sum[b, a] + C * mean[a, b] * mean[b, a]
```
As you can now see, the `top[a, b] == top[b, a]` as well, meaning that the correlation matrix itself should also be symmetric.

> If I want to support .hex files, do I need to pass some configuration to the build system - to output hex files? Should the plugin somehow require this from the mbed_base.py? Is that even part of htrun?

Htrun doesn't know anything about the build system really, it just knows about targets and binaries. I think just checking the file extension of `image_path` would be sufficient to make the behavior conditional (as @desowin suggested).

> If I want to support .bin files, I need to get the start address of the internal flash from the MCU. Is there any such mapping? I don't want to use mbed-ls here only to detect the target information. Is there a simple way to do this?

I don't really have much of an idea here unfortunately. Htrun doesn't have access to the start address of ROM at the moment. It only initially supported flashing via the Mbed drive (USB MSD) as @desowin mentioned, so this information was never integrated into this tool.

One idea we've had for the future is to consolidate around [pyocd](https://github.com/mbedmicro/pyOCD) as our flashing and reset mechanism. It has growing target support. There are actually 2 problems:
1. Connection is closed with "Protocol out of sync". I suspect I know the root cause but I am intentionally not fixing it now. When I deploy this version, this log message will tell me whether I'm right about it and I will know for sure. That's when I fix it.
2. When connection is closed, it leaves in_flight_requests hanging.
The timeline is as follows:
- send() is called, sends a message
- before future is saved to `in_flight_requests`, another thread calls `close()` (such as because of problem number 1)
- `close()` terminates all futures in `in_flight_requests`, but there are none there
- thread which called `send()` stores the future to disconnected connection's `in_flight_requests`
- the future remains unresolved forever = worker deadlocks

We don't want to use this line for two reasons:
1) We should never use Six, as Future is a better and safer version. For example, `six.StringIO` behaves differently between Py2 vs Py3, which is bad. The only exception is for our checker code, as it cannot use Future due to being a universal wheel.
2) We should never use `string_types`, as it contradicts Py3 goal of splitting unicode from bytes. Instead, try to clarify the API, and if we really do need to support both types, use the idiom `isinstance(my_stringy_thing, (str, bytes))`.

I've pushed a fix that removes the compile guard.  Before the fix and without the guard, the following step of the compile failed:
```
g++ ... -DMSHADOW_USE_CUDNN=1 ... -c cudnn_algoreg.cc
```
The problem is that the cudnn_algoreg-inl.h and cudnn_algoreg.cc files look at MXNET_USE_CUDNN, not MSHADOW_USE_CUDNN.  The header file ./include/mxnet/libinfo.h has the vital missing line that equates the two:
```
#define MXNET_USE_CUDNN MSHADOW_USE_CUDNN
```
So this was a case of not adhering to the rule 'include what you use.'  The header file ./src/common/cuda_utils.h was referring to MXNET_USE_CUDNN, but not including any header file that would ensure libinfo.h was included.  Now fixed.

Now that MSHADOW is part of MXNET and no longer a submodule, is there a benefit to consolidating the dual naming as in {MSHADOW,MXNET}_USE_CUDNN?

You cannot compare string with Enum this way. Such comparison will always return `False.`
```python
missing_type = 'Zero'
missing_type == _MissingType.ZERO  # False
```

I believe the opposite is true. 

The range-based for loop has been introduced to remove code like `prefixes_copy[i]`. Such code is a major cause of crashes, so every time I read this I have to wonder "Can this code crash ?". This makes it less readable. 
Then, I go back to the loop statement and read that `i` can go up to `num_returned-1`. I don't know, just by reading this line, whether this value is less than `prefixes_copy.size()`. So, I have to go go back again to check that. Again less readable.
In addition, it is possible that someone changes the definition of `num_returned` and it may cause a crash later in the loop.

In contrast, range-based for loops almost never crash. People used to complain about C/C++ segfaulting too often. Modern C++ has removed most causes of segfaults, and that's why ranged based for loops are almost always preferred.

Regarding the condition + break, it makes it clear that `outputs` is limited to a maximum size of `num_returned`, which is what the name "num_returned" means. With previous code, it was slightly less obvious.

`(?s)` is an inline flag, which is the same as `re.DOTALL` ([ref](https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html#re.DOTALL)). This is needed because I add a `$` to make this schema pattern to match the whole commit message instead of matching the first line only. If the flag is not set, `\n\n.*` only allows one line of message body.

You can use the following code to verify that behavior:
```python
import re

PATTERN = (
    r"(?s)"
    r"(build|ci|docs|feat|fix|perf|refactor|style|test|chore|revert|bump)"  # type
    r"(\(\S+\))?!?:"  # scope
    r"( [^\n\r]+)"  # subject
    r"((\n\n.*)|(\s*))?$"
)

# Shouldn't be `None`
print(
    re.match(
        PATTERN,
        (
            "refactor(docs): remove README.rst and use docs/README.md\n\n"
            "By removing README.rst, we no longer need to maintain "
            "two document with almost the same content\n"
            "Github can read docs/README.md as README for the project."
        ),
    ),
)
# Should be None
print(
    re.match(PATTERN, "feat: an awesome feature\nwith an ill-formated commit message"),
)
```

`TypeObject` And `TypeInformation` are terms defined in the [DDS-XTypes](https://www.omg.org/spec/DDS-XTypes/1.3/PDF) standard.
`TypeObject` contains the full information about the type, needed to dynamically build it (at the remote endpoint, e.g the sniffer). `TypeInformation` is an approach to reduce discovery traffic, sending lighter identifiers, and only interested `DomainParticipants` will request the full `TypeObject` (using additionally built domain entities).

Since we **are** interested in the `TypeObject`, using the `TypeInformation` is redundant and wasteful.
In current Fast-DDS implementation if both `TypeInformation` and `TypeObject` are sent the subscriber to the topic will not be matched (probably need to use additional services like `TypeLookupService` to match)

No, the parameter `tag` is mandatory. So we will have a tag nonetheless.
If we provide a `msg`, it will all depend on whether we defined the parameters `annotated` or `signed`. Because if `msg` is defined, the `or msg is None` part will be False and it will depend on `_opt`, which depends on `annotated` or `signed`.

Assuming we HAVE defined `msg` and `tag=MY_TAG`:
1. If we define `annotated = False` or `signed = False`, `_opt` will remain an empty string.
   - This means the condition `_opt == ""` will be True, so: `git tag "MY_TAG"`
2. If we define either `annotated = True` or `signed = True`, `_opt` won't be an empty string anymore.
   - This means the condition `_opt == ""` will be False. Since `msg` is also defined, `msg is None` will be False as well. So, the resulting command will be, for example: `git tag -a MY_TAG -m {msg}`

In normal operation, on Windows, we both "convert" ANSI characters to win32 API calls (thus displaying colors in the terminal), AND then "strip" the ANSI chars from the output (to prevent the ANSI characters from being echoed to the output in the terminal)

So whether a user wants to see colors or not, on Windows we always want to strip the ANSI from the output.

Three other options that I have played around with:

1. Provide the GCS client to the `LessorPicker`, and move the factory function logic into `GetBestNodeAddrForTask`.
2. Provide the GCS client to the `CoreWorkerDirectTaskSubmitter`, change the interface of `LessorPickerInterface` to `GetBestNodeForTask` (removing `GetBestNodeAddrForTask` from `LessorPicker`), and move the factory function logic to the call site of `GetBestNodeForTask` in the direct task submitter.
3. Provide this factory function to the `CoreWorkerDirectTaskSubmitter` and change the interface of `LessorPickerInterface` to `GetBestNodeForTask` (removing `GetBestNodeAddrForTask` from `LessorPicker`).

I think that (1) and (2) expose an unnecessary amount of the GCS API surface to `LessorPicker` and `CoreWorkerDirectTaskSubmitter`, respectively, and is harder to test (would have to mock the `GcsClient` in the `LessorPicker`/`CoreWorkerDirectTaskSubmitter` tests), so I went with the factory injection pattern. (3) decouples all GCS/RPC address considerations from the `LessorPicker` (it would only deal with `NodeID`s) and doesn't add a lot of testing complexity, at the cost of moving the `NodeID` --> `rpc::Address` conversion up one abstraction level. I think that (3) might be a better route?

This was an oversight that's getting fixed in #591. What `to_numpy` is supposed to do is convert it to a NumPy array (i.e. bring it from the GPU to main memory).

The handling of GPU arrays isn't done: there are `// DERIVE` comments throughout the C++ codebase indicating spots where we're supposed to identify whether the buffers are all main memory (`kernel::lib::cpu`), all on GPUs (`kernel::lib::cuda`), or mixed (raising an exception). The Python side was mostly made NumPy/CuPy generic, but you found a missed case. I should do a more thorough sweep, like the one I'm doing for RegularArray `size == 0` in #590. We also need to get more meaningful tests for the kernels (the test cases in kernel-specification.yml haven't been checked for exhaustiveness) before implementing all the kernels in CUDA.

That's why I'm not too concerned about hard-coding the name `"cuda."` at this time: the sweep that will homogenize all of these things and make them ready for TensorFlow, PyTorch tensors, and JAX in addition to just CuPy is in the future. (That is, we're not regressing an already correct thing.)

So this is pretty tricky to model. We really have two types of sources fields going on - cargo.toml and the normal .RS files. It is very useful for those to be seen as completely separate fields, so that we can do things like be confident every single file is a rust file and it's safe to feed into rustc and rustfmt.

The issue is that we strongly assume there is only one sources field per target type. That is pretty crucial to have the target API Works when you say tgt.get(SourcesField). The safer thing to do would have been to have that return a tuple, but we intentionally return a single field or none.

There is one workaround I can think of, which is to use `SecondaryOwnerMixin` with a dedicated field for the cargo.toml. It is hacky, but I think would work.

@yassineAlouini From what I see you've reverted the code that reused estimations from `ops.boxes` and now use the a modified version of the loss at [Detectron2](https://github.com/facebookresearch/detectron2/blob/dfe8d368c8b7cc2be42c5c3faf9bdcc3c08257b1/detectron2/layers/losses.py#L5). BTW we should add a reference in the source code similar to [this](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/main/torchvision/ops/focal_loss.py#L34), to indicate the original source.

Concerning the casting question, note that in `ops.boxes` the [_upcast method](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/main/torchvision/ops/boxes.py#L218-L223) is primarily there to handle overflows of low precision values that might overflow. The main concern is integers (because those methods need to support them). In the case of losses, the [_upcast method](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/main/torchvision/ops/giou_loss.py#L7-L11) works a bit different as it converts everything to floats (we don't handle integers in losses). This is crucial because some of the area estimation operations will overflow in `torch.float16`.

```
>>> torch.finfo(torch.float16)
finfo(resolution=0.001, min=-65504, max=65504, eps=0.000976562, tiny=6.10352e-05, dtype=float16)
```

So I think the safe thing to do here is to follow the same approach as in gIoU and upcast. I think we should move the method `_upcast` to `_utils` so it can be shared. This is going to be useful also for the cIoU PR that @abhi-glitchhg is working on.

Adding more context here to illustrate the usage of these two-lines code:

When we stop an EventHubConsumer(EventProcessor) in the async world, chances are that an async task (the coroutine method `_receive`) is cancelled before it's executed. In this case, the program would hang when `EventProcessor.stop()` is called. -- The task never gets a chance to be removed from the dict.

The self._consumers[partition_id] serves as a flag whether  `_receive` has been executed.
If it's cancelled while the consumer is not set, then we should proactively remove the task here as the `_receive` doesn't get a chance to execute itself.
If it's cancelled while the consumer is already set, then the coroutine method (`receive`) itself will be responsible for removing the task.

The question is, if there are 8 partitions and prefetch is 300. By retrieving only one here, the EP will be working like, p0 1 event, p1 1 event, ... p7 1 event, then p0 1 event again ...

If we retrieve all events here, the EP will be working like p0 300 events, p1 300 events, ... p7 300 events, then p0 300 events again ...

The lint failure is because on python 2, print is a built-in keyword used like this `print 'Hello World!'`, while on python 3, this is a built-in function called like this `print('Hello World!').

And pylint is executed with python 2 in our CI, so it complains about the superfluous parenthesis on that version. 

Add `from __future__ import print_function` to instruct python 2 interpreter to also use print as a built-in function, and pylint will stop to complain. 

Or you can also just remove the two prints here. Most of the time printing from unit tests is not useful. Either the test passes and pytest will hide every output, or what is printed is significant for the test success, and an assertion should be use instead. Printing is then useful only for debugging purpose when a test fails.

I believe I fixed this. I can show you the patch if you want, but I basically:

1. `import functools`
2. change `fakeSite` from a fixture, where all the args and kwargs need to be fixtures, to a function with kwargs we can fill in:

```
def fakeSite(url, request, expected_url=None, expected_body=None):
    if expected_url is not None:
        assert urllib.parse.urlunsplit(url) == expected_url
    if expected_body is not None:
        assert json.loads(request.body) == expected_body
    return ""
```

Then, to use:

```
def test_no_args_no_input(client):
    site = functools.partial(
        fakeSite,
        expected_url='https://tc-tests.example.com/api/fake/v1/no_args_no_input',
        # if we have an expected body, also pass `expected_body={...},`
    )
    with httmock.HTTMock(site):
         client.no_args_no_input()
```

For the `json.loads` values, set `expected_body`. Essentially, we're creating another function based on `fakeSite`, but with `expected_body` and `expected_url` optionally pre-populated.

I recommend to remove that early check here:

* It makes the code harder to understand.
* It's another code path that could introduce bugs if the code is ever changed.
* I don't think it will measurably improve performance. It could even degrade performance, because it introduces extra function calls in the common case, where no numeric types are part of the union. But we would need some metrics to prove either case.

I don't think there's a strong reason overall to prefer either approach (there are pros and cons to both with respect to complexity vs duplication/generality), but I do think the problem is adequately solved. In general, Steinhardt is the first time that I'm finding this problem, and it's specifically because in `computeAve` we use the indexer inside like 4 nested for loops _and_ the indexing is the only operation in there. I haven't noticed the problem in any other changes. We can definitely revisit this if we start seeing the slowdown more often, but for now I don't think it's worth overreacting to this one use-case.

I would consider this difference to be similar to the difference between directly indexing into vs use `at` for `std::vector`, there are tradeoffs to both approaches and I think it's OK to just have the options available.

Because of that, i dont believe making kernprof tied to line_profiler is a good idea

Two things here:
- The logging formatter will add the "DEBUG:" part for us; it shouldn't be in the message body. (It's very possible we aren't consistent about this in ophyd. The logging overall needs a thorough review.)
- A bit of Python logging best practice... Although we love and encourage f-strings in general, for log messages in particular the old-style string templating is recommended because the logging framework can ensure that the templating will only be performed if that log message is actually issued. That is, if there is nothing handling DEBUG-level log messages, then you won't pay the performance overhead of building this string. Note that the values are passed as arguments to `debug`, like `debug('...', a, b)` not `'debug(...' % (a, b))`. The formatting is deferred and only happens if it needs to. ```
```
def calculate_checksum(origin_json_data: str,
                    filename: str,
                    checksum: str):
    """Calculate checksum and validate that it passes."""
    if checksum == 'md5':
        hash_type = hashlib.md5
    elif checksum == 'sha256':
        hash_type = hashlib.sha256
    # Calculate returned hash
    with open(filename, 'rb') as file_to_check:
        json_data = file_to_check.read()
        origin_hash = hash_type(origin_json_data).hexdigest()
        returned_hash = hash_type(json_data).hexdigest()
        # Compare original hashkey in dict with calculated
        if origin_hash != returned_hash:
            print('origin_hash: ', origin_hash)
            print('returned_hash: ', returned_hash)
            raise exceptions.ClientError(
                'Checksum failed. File not correctly downloaded.')
        return f'({checksum}) checksum succesful: ({filename})'
```

You can have a buffer which is not direct in this code, but it won't have a memoryview currently.  I left the stubs in case we wanted to implement indirect buffers as well.   

But I am very reluctant to go down that path.   If we implement both direct and java array backed buffers on this interface, then we will suffer the problem that java array buffers can't be made into read/write buffers because the JNI pinning restrictions.   As it stands you can make a memory buffer for a direct buffer which will fail for an indirect buffer, but if it is indirect you can call the array method which will give you the memory buffer with array rules.

I believe this is the "best" compromise in terms of design.   Arrays have one set of rules and (direct) Buffers have a different set.    Obviously some docs would help.   The rules are pretty obvious at the implementation level given the JNI calls, but may be surprising for those not familiar with JNI.

This command `az identity federated-credential create` acts on **managed identity**: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/workload-identities/workload-identity-federation-create-trust-user-assigned-managed-identity?pivots=identity-wif-mi-methods-azcli

`az ad app federated-credential create` (#23122) acts on **AAD application**: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/workload-identities/workload-identity-federation-create-trust?pivots=identity-wif-apps-methods-azcli

However, they expose different command interfaces:

- `az identity federated-credential create` uses separated arguments `--issuer`, `--subject` and `--audiences`
- `az ad app federated-credential create` uses `--parameters` which includes `issuer`, `subject` and `audiences` in a JSON payload. This decision was made in email thread: _federatedIdentityCredentials's implementation in Azure CLI_

In my opinion, this causes inconsistency and increases users' learning cost.

I do not like @staticmethod because it's not a concept in many languages. A compiler can figure out what's a static method and there should be no need to declare it. If I want to call other class methods, I like having `cls` already available as a variable so I don't have to distract myself by adding data flow to receive the class name. If something is truly promised to be a static method, I just make it a function. There is no need to go through the syntax of calling it indirect through a class. It could be called by any class and it's weird to hide it somewhere that makes it syntactically hard for other things to get to it.  If it's part of a class, then it should have access to the class methods.

Also, as a practical matter, Python fusses if something is an instance method but could be a static method but it does not fuss if it's a class method and could be a static method, probably because it wants to accommodate exactly the programming style that I use.

```python
import httpx
import anyio
import quart
import quart_trio

import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

blueprint = quart.Blueprint("blueprint", __name__)


class MyCrashError(Exception):
    pass


@blueprint.route("/")
async def index():
    async def crash():
        raise MyCrashError

    quart.current_app.nursery.start_soon(crash)
    return b""


def create_app():
    app = quart_trio.QuartTrio(__name__)
    app.register_blueprint(blueprint)
    return app

    async with anyio.create_task_group() as tg:

        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            await client.get("http://localhost:5000/")
            (await client.get("http://localhost:5000/")).raise_for_status()


anyio.run(amain, backend="trio")
```

In general I would advise against using `capture: true` here. This is intended for when you have to deal with external tools that do not provide an option to write to a file. Writing the file yourself is much better.

Use of this option causes meson to run your gen_loader.py script in a python-based wrapper (`meson --internal exe`) that captures stdout and writes it to a file. It makes the execution logging of `ninja -v` harder to read and the overall build times slower due to needing to run a wrapper script.

DRY in tests is an antipattern as far as I'm concerned -- tests should be easy to read and obvious at a glance what behaviour is being tested (without indirection)

here's how I'd write this test:

```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ('s', 'expected_groups'),
    (
        ('[isort]\nknown_third_party=\n', ('', '')),
        ('[isort]\nknown_third_party = foo\n', (' ', ' ')),
        ('[isort]\nknown_third_party\t=\tfoo\n', ('\t', '\t')),
        ('[isort]\nknown_third_party =\nknown_wat=wat\n', (' ', '')),
    ),
)
def test_known_third_party_re(s, expected_groups):
    match = THIRD_PARTY_RE.search(s)
    assert match
    assert match.groups() == expected_groups


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ('s', 'expected_groups'),
    (
        ('[isort]\nknown_other=\n', ('other', '')),
        ('[isort]\nknown_other = foo\n', ('other', 'foo')),
        ('[isort]\nknown_other\t=\tfoo\n', ('other', 'foo')),
        ('[isort]\nknown_other =\nknown_third_party=wat\n', ('other', '')),
    ),
)
def test_known_other_re(s, expected_groups):
    match = KNOWN_OTHER_RE.search(s)
    assert match
    assert match.groups() == expected_groups
```

In principle, I agree with you that it could subclass from DeprecationWarning.

However, by default the DeprecationWarning is filtered out:
```python
>>> import warnings
>>> def deprecated():
...     warnings.warn("deprecated", DeprecationWarning)
... 
>>> deprecated()
>>> def user_deprecated():
...     warnings.warn("user_deprecated", UserWarning)
... 
>>> user_deprecated()
__main__:2: UserWarning: user_deprecated

```
And I find that not very useful if you want to give someone heads up that they need to change something.

Also, with a custom instance of a warning, the user can filter based on that specific warning instance.

```python
with warnings.catch_warnings():
    warnings.simplefilter("ignore", ProjDeprecationWarning)
    ...
```

We need to always raise these errors.

First reason is we can't assume CARTOframes is going to be used just in Jupyter notebooks, so an app using CARTOframes would still need to react to exceptions in CARTO.

The second reason is that `CartoException` can be a `CartoRateLimitException` which contains info about when to retry the query and so. This is needed by caller of this API if they want to implement a more robust rate-limit proof library. Otherwise these queries might be affected randomly by rate-limits with no way of reacting to them.

Apart from that, we should include it in the `raises` section of the API docs. Also it's important to note, that these queries might be affected by database timeouts as well, which in some cases can be quite low to run any arbitrary query. For long running queries, we should redirect users to a way of running those queries with the COPY API (which on the other hand is more restricted in terms of rate limits).

I am not sure that calling `Encrypt` here is actually correct, in general.  The issue is that the underlying platform may not support encrypting arbitrary amounts of data (for example, the API called by the go-cloud implementation for AWS ends up just calling the https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/APIReference/API_Encrypt.html API, which is limited to 4K of data. 

I think that instead, when we first create the secrets provider, we should generate a 256 bit key, and encrypt that and store it as part of the state, in addition to the URL we need to pass to the keeper.  When we preforming an encryption operation, we just use that key as the key for the same AES encryption logic we do for the passphrase based secrets manager.

In `FromState` we would use the URL to call `OpenKeeper`, which we'd use the decrypt the key we generated.  I think we should be able to reuse much of `config.NewSymmetricCrypter` here.

> Slightly bettr imo would be to port to python_binary targets first and then when pants can be python 3 only, do the fun stuff.

Porting Pants itself to Py3 won't have any impact on whether we should implement our own color functions vs. use the colors library. We can today run the scripts either via `./pants run` or by directly invoking them, and Py3 compatibility works fine as is, even if we were to link it to our 3rd party deps and/or Pants src code.

The idea with solely depending on stdlib is it gives us the option to run directly, rather than requiring us to use `./pants run`. I think this is desirable for two reasons:

1) Closer alignment with how we run bash scripts. It shouldn't matter whether the build-support script is written in Bash or Python—that's an implementation detail. Requiring the user to use `./pants run` for one but not even allowing `./pants run` for the other (because Pants doesn't support bash targets) is surprising to me.

2) Ensures we have no dependencies on Pants, as some of the scripts are (will be) used to lint either the bash scripts or source code. For example, I'll soon be adding a [`shellcheck.py` script](https://github.com/pantsbuild/setup/blob/67ac92821b8031626b23b34a46494338b9de3152/build-support/shellcheck.py). If we only allow that script to be ran via `./pants run`, then let's say we have an issue with the `./pants` script that `shellcheck.py` is meant to check, the script won't be able to properly run shellcheck because the `./pants run` part will fail.
   * Although, now that I write this out, I'm less convinced this is important to maintain the separation. If `./pants run` fails because of an issue with the `./pants` script, presumably the error will print and the user will know they need to fix it? The only benefit I could see is if shellcheck would help to diagnose the issue. 

--

The duplication of `common.sh` is intentional. Imo, it should be just as easy to develop a Python script as a bash script, meaning we surface the same util code. There will be tasks that are better for Bash (e.g. installing Pyenv) and better for Python (e.g. `check_pants_pex_abi`, which started as Bash actually)—it should be well supported for the developer to choose the best language for the task at hand.

--

We now again have competing operator implementations which are chosen at compile time - numpyGpu vs cutensor. This creates another situation where from a user perspective, we are adding another magic knob which improves performance but is pretty hidden. As a product, we should make mxnet smart enough to figure these things out at runtime - run with cutensor if available and fall back to numpy implementation (our own) if not available. But adding even more compile time branching sounds totally user-unfriendly in my opinion. 

In the end, this might totally worth for a benchmark scenario or a situation where somebody knows exactly what's happening in every detail of mxnet, but the standard user should get a good Performance without having to know every single detail of every single operator and which build flags to choose - this is a more fundamental thing.

My recommendation is to use `default_delete<bool[]>` as the simplest portable version.  Or possibly switch to `unique_ptr`, since supposedly it has overloads for array deletion.  But you still need to use `bool[]` for the template type.


Remember that decorators don't really have any magic... when you see:

```python
@_public
def fn(...):
    ...
```

that's just a shorthand for 

```python
def fn(...):
    ...
fn = _public(fn)
```

So if `_public` is a simple no-op like in our example above, then this becomes:

```python
def fn(...):
    ...
fn = fn
```

So it makes sense that a no-op decorator doesn't have any effect, because it literally has no effect at all after the module finishes importing.

1. The typical use case of is for the raylet/bakend to send a signal (i.e., SIG_FAULT) on the behalf of an actor/task that has failed, i.e., `signal.send(signal.Signal(SIG_FAULT), source_id = a)`. In this case we might want to actually disable fault tolerance for the actors/tasks.

For the other place, I don't think the call to os.path.abspath() is needed and the check `if logdir in assets_dir: logdir = assets_dir` looks incorrect to me because it would include cases where the logdir is a prefix of the assets_dir, which should be true in all cases, so it would break the case where the checkpoints are in the assets dir.

My understanding of the original issue you raised was that calling `logdir = _assets_dir_to_logdir(assets_dir)` is incorrect in the case where there are no asset dirs, so self.logdir gets used as the only asset dir, with the following code in the configs method:

```py
# If there are no summary event files, the projector should still work,
# treating the `logdir` as the model checkpoint directory.
if not run_path_pairs:
   run_path_pairs.append(('.', self.logdir))
```

In particular, calling _assets_dir_to_logdir() will do the wrong thing if self.logdir incidentally contains a path segment `/plugins/` even though it's not a plugins asset directory.

My proposal was to fix this by skipping _assets_dir_to_logdir() if the assets dir equals self.logdir exactly, since that's the precise case where it shouldn't be called.

ah, this was confusing.  We used to get the task back directly from the asyncio call, but now we get back a future that knows about the task and acts as a proxy.  When you call `tsk.cancel()` it is left in a "please cancel the next time you get a chance" state (as it arranges to throw the `CancelledError` down into the wrapped co-ro).  However, when you call `future.cancel()` the proxy forwards cancel call and uncontitionally marks itself as cancelled.  Meanwhile, the underlying task may have delayed (or entirely eaten as we do) the cancellation and still be a going concern.   This effectively de-couples the future from the task which breaks things.

The previous logic was "both could be used but had to match".  The current logic is "--os can't be used". This is a breaking change. A more logical way would be for `--os` and `--platform` to reference the same variable, but have `--os` deprecated in favor of `--platform`.  It would look something like:

```Python
with self.argument_context(scope) as c:
  c.argument('platform', options_list=['--platform', c.deprecate(target='--os', redirect='--platform', hide=True)])
```

In this way, --platform no longer appears, but does work, and fills the same value as using `--os` would.

It was not obvious to me that `module.__dict__` is sufficient. For
instance, if `type(module)` is not `types.ModuleType` but rather a
subtype of that type, then values defined on `type(module)` will not
appear in `module.__dict__`. We _do_ use custom module types for
TensorBoard `LazyModule`s, though they don’t export additional symbols.

But this does in fact appear to be consistent with the native behavior
of `from … import *`, per `Python/ceval.c:import_all_from` in the
CPython source:
<https://github.com/wchargin/cpython/blob/1f512b9a344b5053c013cf0a116b3562d426ef2b/Python/ceval.c#L5099-L5100>

> That is mainly my question, i.e. whether OT users need to be aware of PSA and manage key refs at next layer (what are the security implication of this).

I think that OT users should have access to both APIs: To use key literals, or to use PSA key refs.

Most probably most users would like to use key literals, because that's easier.

However, there may be very secure application running OpenThread. OpenThread should work in Non-Secure Processing Environment. And in very secure applications, key literals shall never be present in NSPE. That's why in such application keys are retrieved from some source (could be out-of-band commissioning) and stored in Secure Processing Environment. OpenThread API user would have access only to key reference, but not to its value. To configure OpenThread, API user would need to pass key reference to OpenThread.

But for sure, we don't want to support this ultra-secure scenario in this PR. However, I think we should keep in mind that such scenario may arise and have public APIs prepared for this. One example of API preparation could be an extendable union containing only key literal at the moment, but could be extended with PSA key reference in the future.

The `TypeError` vs `ValueError`, here is ambiguous:
- We can say it's the right type (a file like object) but the wrong value (opened in text mode).
- We can say it's the wrong type (a `TextIOWrapper` instead of a `BufferedReader`).

For a normal user, the first one is easy to understand, the doc only speak about "file like objects". Only by looking at the (current) implementation of `open` we spot those are different types.

In your example with `/dev/null` it's less ambigous : the `write` method on a `BufferedReader` expects a `bytes` object, if you give an `str` instead, it's the wrong type. (it's the argument to `write` that causes the exception, not the type of the file, even if by changing the type of the file the exception dissapear... I agree).

When writing it in the first place I remember digging and doodling to choose ValueError vs TypeError, and made my final decision by running:

```
$ git -C ../cpython grep 'should be opened in binary'
Lib/asyncio/base_events.py:            raise ValueError("file should be opened in binary mode")
[...]
Lib/socket.py:            raise ValueError("file should be opened in binary mode")
[...]
```

So choosing `ValueError` is consistent with the Python stdlib, and it's "developer-friendly" by saying "It's the right type: a file-like object, but the wrong value".

While doodling around it, I found another error, I only tested with a file readable in text mode, by adding a test with a binary file I get a UnicodeDecodeError on the read before.

The outer function name can be retrieved using following [PEP 3155](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3155/#example-with-nested-functions):

```py
import inspect


def get_printer(msg):
    def printer():
        print(msg)
    return printer


p = get_printer("Hello")
print(p.__qualname__)
print(inspect.getclosurevars(p))
```

Output:

```
get_printer.<locals>.printer
ClosureVars(nonlocals={'msg': 'Hello'}, globals={}, builtins={'print': <built-in function print>}, unbound=set())
```

Distro packages MUST NOT have a compiled schema as this is handled globally outside of any package. For the same reason that distro packages must not run `update-desktop-database` or `update-mime-database` or other such commands.

The traditional way to detect that this should not be done, is to only do it when DESTDIR is empty. But existing distro packages are relying on using `make install COMPILE_SCHEMAS=0` which is opt in and anyone using that is definitely not going to be surprised that they need to compile the schemas themselves.

Choose either solution, but please restore the ability for distros to package Guake. :)

For `Ping` call, sent during reconnection, we don't want to trigger reconnection again. So call the callback directly to make the `Ping` RPC call fails immediately.
2. Put retry into `gcs_service_failure_detected_`'s callback. We want to keep the same logic with synchronous reconnection: retry the RPC when reconnection succeed or failed.

Thanks for that feedback, I was thinking about this also.  Here is a question relating to possible implementations of this.  What is the expected behavior for the case of `samples_like` being empty?

`SampleSet.from_samples` supports empty `samples_like`.  But `SampleSet.from_samples_bqm` does not:

```python
>>> bqm = dimod.BinaryQuadraticModel.from_ising({}, {('a', 'b'): -1})
>>> sampleset = dimod.SampleSet.from_samples_bqm([], bqm)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/timdoyle/src/dimod/dimod/sampleset.py", line 788, in from_samples_bqm
    energies = bqm.energies(samples_like)
  File "/Users/timdoyle/src/dimod/dimod/binary/binary_quadratic_model.py", line 1079, in energies
    return self.data.energies(samples_like, dtype=dtype)
  File "dimod/binary/cybqm/cybqm_template.pyx.pxi", line 378, in dimod.binary.cybqm.cybqm_float64.cyBQM_template.energies
    return np.asarray(self._energies[np.float64_t](samples, labels), dtype=dtype)
  File "dimod/binary/cybqm/cybqm_template.pyx.pxi", line 341, in dimod.binary.cybqm.cybqm_float64.cyBQM_template._energies
    raise ValueError(f"missing variable {self.variables[si]!r} in sample(s)")
ValueError: missing variable 'a' in sample(s)
```

So, the question becomes, should `SampleSet.from_samples_cqm` behave like `SampleSet.from_samples_bqm` (effectively not support empty `samples_like`) or should it behave like `SampleSet.from_samples`?

I am leaning toward the latter because the `ValueError` raised by `SampleSet.from_samples_bqm` seems like an accident rather than intention.  That interpretation raises another question: should we change the behavior of `SampleSet.from_samples_bqm` to support empty `samples_like` also?  I would lean toward "yes" to that also.  In any case, having consistent behavior in handling empty `samples_like` among all three methods seems desirable.

The reason of  these noqas usage across `modin.pandas.base/dataframe/series.py` files is that resulting Modin docstrings in terms of `numpydoc` and `pydocstyle` are not quite correct. For example, docstring for `modin.pandas.DataFrame.fillna` method looks as follows:
```
        Fill NA/NaN values using the specified method.

        Notes
        -----
        See pandas API documentation for `pandas.DataFrame.fillna <https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/version/1.4.1/reference/api/pandas.DataFrame.fillna.html>`_ for more.
```
This docstring doesn't have `Parameters`, `Returns` sections, so we need these noqas to pass `doc_checker` tests.
The idea of usage of such approach (short summary + link to pandas API) is that Modin and pandas high-level API is completely similar and there is no need to make unneeded duplication and Modin methods docstrings should contain only Modin specific information (like supported parameters data).

The exception won't occur unless there's a bug in functions that the `get_chunks()` is using.

The `new_chunk_offset` is computed the following way:
```python
new_chunk_offset = (
    subdivided_slices[biggest_chunk_idx + 1]
    - subdivided_slices[biggest_chunk_idx]
) // 2
```
The only way of getting it equal to zero is to have the slices difference equal to 1. The `subdivided_slices` describes the chunk offsets (global start-stop positions), meaning that it has the following properties:
1. The sequence is monotonically increasing (`arr[i] < arr[i+1]`)
2. Its length is not more than (num_rows + 1), as there can't be more chunks than the number of rows.
3. The sum of differences equals to `num_rows` (`sum(arr[i+1] - arr[i])`). This is met, as the difference describes the chunks lengths and the sum of chunk lengths has to be equal to `num_rows`.

Considering these requirements, if the biggest `subdivided_slices` difference is 1, then all of the differences are equal to 1, meaning that there's already `num_rows` amount of chunks each of them consist of one element. An intention to create one more chunk, means that the function's requirement wasn't met (`n_chunks <= self.num_rows`).

So hitting this internal-error exception should be impossible, as there's already a [check that verifies function's requirements](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/854c91a4638a385b6ab23a6bfa89221e92d0921d/modin/experimental/core/execution/native/implementations/omnisci_on_native/exchange/dataframe_protocol/dataframe.py#L279-L282). Hitting it could be possible if there's a bug in either numpy or other protocol methods that are used by `.get_chunks()`.

Yeah so the conclusion here:

The need for a forksafe lock would be to avoid deadlocks. Deadlocks occur when the process forks while a lock is acquired by a thread and since the thread is not carried into the child process the lock is forever locked.

There is the chance that this can happen in the following situation:

If the StatsProcessor acquires the lock and the process forks then the lock will be permanently locked in the child. This is fine assuming we reset the processor before `on_span_finish` is called but the fork check we do in the library is done on span start, not finish.

Thus it's possible that in the child process, before a span is started, a span is finished which would result in a deadlock.

So we might actually want to use a forksafe lock here!

```suggestion
Why is this so? Well, ``start_soon()`` returns as soon as it has scheduled the new task to start running. The flow of execution in the parent then continues on to exit the ``with move_on_after(TIMEOUT):`` block, at which point Trio forgets about the timeout entirely. In order for the timeout to apply to the child task, Trio must be able to tell that its associated cancel scope will stay open for at least as long as the child task is executing. And Trio can only know that for sure if the cancel scope block is outside the nursery block.

You might wonder why Trio can't just remember "this task should be cancelled in ``TIMEOUT`` seconds", even after the ``with move_on_after(TIMEOUT):`` block is gone. The reason has to do with :ref:`how cancellation is implemented <cancellation>`. Recall that cancellation is represented by a `Cancelled` exception, which eventually needs to be caught by the cancel scope that caused it. (Otherwise, the exception would take down your whole program!) In order to be able to cancel the child tasks, the cancel scope has to be able to "see" the `Cancelled` exceptions that they raise -- and those exceptions come out of the ``async with open_nursery()`` block, not out of the call to ``start_soon()``.

If you want a timeout to apply to one task but not another, then you need to put the cancel scope in that individual task's function -- ``child()``, in this example.
```

The benefit of using `Bytes` to represent a byte array instead of just using builtin classes `bytearray` or `bytes` is that it allows us to write conditions in a easier way. 
Bytes automatically convert strings when comparing, which allows us to write filters like:
```python
p.wpan.src64 == "00:11:22:33:44:55:66:77"
```
Note `p.wpan.src64` returns an `ExtAddr` value which is a subclass of `Bytes`. 

It also allows us to compare IP6 addresses easily. 
```python
p.ip6.src[:8] == "fd00:0d8b:0000:0000"
```

Types like `ExtAddr`, `EthAddr`, `Ipv6Addr` are subclasses of `Bytes`. They are used for layer fields of known types.

Sorry, description may not be obvious. The point is the following:
- we refactor duplicated code into a method `_compute_output_size`
- we remove `max_size` arg from private functional modules: `F_t` and `F_pil` where we can do what we want
- For v1 API no breakage as we keep public functional `resize` method without any change in the signature.
- For v2 API, we will implement things in the following way:
  - For image tensor, we reuse `_compute_output_size` and call as previously `F_t.resize`
```python
def resize_image_tensor(
    image: torch.Tensor,
    size: List[int],
    interpolation: InterpolationMode = InterpolationMode.BILINEAR,
    max_size: Optional[int] = None,
    antialias: Optional[bool] = None,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    num_channels, old_height, old_width = get_dimensions_image_tensor(image)
    size = _compute_output_size((old_height, old_width), size=size, max_size=max_size)
    new_height, new_width = size
    batch_shape = image.shape[:-3]
    return _FT.resize(
        image.reshape((-1, num_channels, old_height, old_width)),
        size=size,
        interpolation=interpolation.value,
        antialias=antialias,
    ).reshape(batch_shape + (num_channels, new_height, new_width))
```
  
  - For pillow images, we do the same thing: we reuse `_compute_output_size` and call as previously `F_pil.resize`
  
```python
def resize_image_pil(
    img: PIL.Image.Image,
    size: Union[Sequence[int], int],
    interpolation: InterpolationMode = InterpolationMode.BILINEAR,
    max_size: Optional[int] = None,
) -> PIL.Image.Image:
    if isinstance(size, int):
        size = [size, size]
    # Explicitly cast size to list otherwise mypy issue: incompatible type "Sequence[int]"; expected "List[int]"
    size: List[int] = list(size)
    size = _compute_output_size(img.size[::-1], size=size, max_size=max_size)
    return _FP.resize(img, size, interpolation=pil_modes_mapping[interpolation])
```

  - For bounding boxes we can now implement `max_size` option (which is not the case today):
```python
def resize_bounding_box(
    bounding_box: torch.Tensor, size: List[int], image_size: Tuple[int, int], max_size: Optional[int] = None
) -> torch.Tensor:
    old_height, old_width = image_size
    size = _compute_output_size(image_size, size=size, max_size=max_size)
    new_height, new_width = size
    ratios = torch.tensor((new_width / old_width, new_height / old_height), device=bounding_box.device)
    return bounding_box.view(-1, 2, 2).mul(ratios).view(bounding_box.shape)
```

> You have to declare all transitions for a state in one single line.

**question**: Why do you have added this statement? Can you give me an example?

I think that there's not that blocks someone from writing on multiple lines.

## Example

Consider the [`Guess the number example`](https://python-statemachine.readthedocs.io/en/develop/auto_examples/guess_the_number_machine.html), someone can write the events like this and it also works:

```py
class GuessTheNumberMachine(StateMachine):

    start = State("Start", initial=True)
    low = State("Low")
    high = State("High")
    won = State("Won", final=True)
    lose = State("Lose", final=True)

    guess = lose.from_(low, high, cond="max_guesses_reached")
    guess = guess | won.from_(low, high, cond="guess_is_equal")
    guess = guess | low.from_(low, high, start, cond="guess_is_lower")
    guess = guess | high.from_(low, high, start, cond="guess_is_higher")    
```

Or eve the compact form:

```py
class GuessTheNumberMachine(StateMachine):

    start = State("Start", initial=True)
    low = State("Low")
    high = State("High")
    won = State("Won", final=True)
    lose = State("Lose", final=True)

    guess = lose.from_(low, high, cond="max_guesses_reached")
    guess |= won.from_(low, high, cond="guess_is_equal")
    guess |= low.from_(low, high, start, cond="guess_is_lower")
    guess |= high.from_(low, high, start, cond="guess_is_higher")
    
```

This is poor form, IMO. I watched the video and follow the logic, but sys.exit is more idiomatic to me. The `raise` implies an error. I realise that the exception is how Python works internally, but I believe that it does that to conveniently reuse the try/finally semantics, and not because it should be viewed as an error. It comes across strangely in the case that this is `raise SystemExit(0)` - there, the program is exiting successfully but our code is raising an exception. 

More familiar is a function that never returns, like C's `exit()`. That's also how most (all?) other mainstream languages do it, e.g. [Rust](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/process/fn.exit.html), [Ruby](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/30337972/how-to-tell-a-ruby-program-to-close-itself), [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/api/process.html#processexitcode), [Java](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/system-exit-in-java/), [Go](https://gobyexample.com/exit)...

```suggestion
    def get_ttl(self, key: t.Hashable) -> t.Optional[T_TTL]:
        """
        Return the remaining time to live of a key that has a TTL.

        Args:
            key: Cache key.

        Returns:
            The remaining time to live of `key` or ``None`` if the key doesn't exist or has expired.
        """
        with self._lock:
            if not self._has(key):
                return None

            expire_time = self._expire_times.copy().get(key)
            if expire_time is None:
                return None

            ttl = expire_time - self.timer()
            if ttl <= 0:
                self._delete(key)
                return None

            return ttl
```

I changed two things here.

1) `realloc()` does not `free()` the memory on failures, and I think we shouldn't do that here either. According to the [example in the Lua docs](https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/manual.html#lua_Alloc), Lua uses the plain `realloc()` behaviour. Therefore, I removed the `free()`. I would assume Lua to call into our allocator again in order to free the old pointer on failures, if it decides to free it.

2) The original calculation was `memory_left[0] -= new_size + old_size`, which looked wrong to me. It should add the difference, not subtract the sum.

I just thought of another "evil hack" that avoids the out of place inversion here. There are two places where `paste_alpha_mask` or its inverse is used:

1. `image = (image * inverse_paste_alpha_mask).add_(paste_image * paste_alpha_mask)`
2. `masks = masks * inverse_paste_alpha_mask`

By reordering the first computation to

```py
image = (paste_image * paste_alpha_mask).add_(image * inverse_paste_alpha_mask)
```

we no longer need the `paste_alpha_mask` after `inverse_paste_alpha_mask` is used the first time. Thus, we can perform inplace operations on it:

```py
image = (paste_image * paste_alpha_mask).add_(image * paste_alpha_mask.logical_not_())
```

That turns the 2. statement into

```py
`masks = masks * paste_alpha_mask`
```

Although that looks wrong, it is actually correct since `paste_alpha_mask` at this point contains it inverse.

We consolidate the syncers, i.e. there is either a node syncer (when no cloud syncing) or there is a cloud syncer (and no between-node syncing) - so we never have both at the same time (anymore).

The cloud syncer is created in the trial runner for experiment checkpointing. If no cloud-checkpointing is used, it is no-op.
The node syncer is created in the syncer callback. If cloud-checkpointing is used, it is no-op.

Trial checkpointing (previously using the durable trainable) directly interfaces with the sync clients (not the syncer classes), so does not care about this feature. However for reference, it returns a function-based sync client (e.g. an s3 command) if a string is passed or a default cloud syncer if an upload dir is passed

In the very first comment in the `__main__.py` file I suggested that we simply do not accept daemon address as IP addr/hostname + port number, Instead, we ask the user to supply a complete and valid multi-addr. This way, we can simply store the default multi-addr as a plain string and allow it to be overwritten completely by the CLI argument (if present). We have no business of storing it in pieces or validating in pieces. In order to validate the multi-addr, simply attempt to connect to it by creating a client using the function provided by the IPFS library. If there is any formatting or network issue, the library will through an exception that can be caught and reported. In case of success, we may choose to reuse this client or dispose it. This method does not require us to attempt for many different ways a multi-addr can be formatted, because we won't constructing it piece by piece.
